layer_1_flower.intent: Marzipan calm with a joyful mint finish

layer_2_essential_oil.blend_rationale: Black Pepper carries the dominant beta-caryophyllene. Lemon matches the mint aroma note. Lavender provides Linalool support. Sage rounds the blend with a soft finish.

layer_3_music.spotify_prompt: A low-energy session. Marzipan calm with a joyful mint finish. Genres: ambient, dub, lo-fi hip hop, deep house. Tempo around 65-90 BPM.

layer_4_tea.name: Amaretto Tea
layer_4_tea.description: Mint and almond paste infused chamomile blend
method: Heat 8 oz filtered water to 185°F.
Add chamomile tea bag and steep for 5 minutes.
Add fresh mint leaves and let infuse for 1 minute.
Add almond milk and let infuse for 1 minute.
Strain into a warm teacup.
Stir in honey and serve.

mocktail.name: Amaretto Fizz
method: Fill a rocks glass with large ice cubes.
Combine 2 oz fresh mint leaves and 0.75 oz fresh lemon juice in a shaker with ice.
Add 0.5 oz orgeat syrup.
Shake for 8 seconds.
Strain into glass over fresh ice.
Top with 2 oz sparkling water and stir gently.
Garnish with mint.

cocktail.name: Amaretto Sour
cocktail.description: Bourbon-based mint and almond paste cocktail
method: Combine 2 oz bourbon, 1.5 oz fresh mint leaves, and 0.75 oz fresh lemon juice in a shaker.
Add 0.5 oz orgeat syrup.
Dry shake for 5 seconds (if using egg white).
Add ice and shake for 10 seconds.
Strain into a chilled coupe.
Garnish with mint.

juice.name: Amaretto Juice
method: Press 2 cups fresh apple through a cold press.
Press 1 green apple through the press.
Add 0.5 inch fresh ginger root to the press.
Combine all juices in a glass.
Add 0.5 oz raw honey and stir until dissolved.
Pour over ice and serve immediately.

wellness_shot.name: Amaretto Shot
ingredients: tiny pinch of hint of cherry
method: Chill a shot glass in the freezer for 2 minutes.
In a mixing glass, combine 1 oz bourbon with 0.75 oz fresh apple juice.
Add 0.25 oz fresh lemon juice.
Stir with ice for 5 seconds.
Strain into the chilled shot glass and serve immediately.

snack.name: Truffle Popcorn with Cracked Pepper
snack.desc: Fresh-popped corn drizzled with truffle oil and fresh-cracked black pepper.

appetizer.name: Blistered Shishito Peppers with Miso Aioli
appetizer.desc: Flash-charred peppers served with white miso and yuzu aioli.

main.name: Duck Confit with Fig Glaze
main.desc: Slow-cooked duck leg with fig reduction, served over creamy polenta.

dessert.name: Fig & Honey Panna Cotta
dessert.desc: Vanilla bean panna cotta with roasted figs and a wildflower honey drizzle.

cheese.name: Brie & Fig Preserves
cheese.desc: Triple-cream Brie with fig preserves, honeycomb, and toasted hazelnuts.

bath_bomb.base: baking soda, citric acid, cornstarch, Epsom salt
bath_bomb.extras: dried flower petals, oat milk powder

shower_steamer.activation: Place on shower floor away from direct stream. Steam activates essential oils for 5-10 minutes of aromatherapy.

layer_10_incense.burn_method: Place on heat-resistant surface in a ventilated room. Allow 30 seconds for the flame to establish before extinguishing to a smolder.
layer_10_incense.ritual_suggestion: Light before the session begins. Let the smoke settle the room while you prepare your marzipan space.

layer_11_environment.spatial_setup: Low seating, soft throws, candles at eye level. Minimize overhead lighting.
layer_11_environment.scent_anchor: Essential oil blend via diffuser, started 30 minutes before session

layer_12_breathwork.timing: Begin with the diffuser. Complete all cycles before consumption.
layer_12_breathwork.guidance_text: Close your eyes. Breathe in through the nose for four counts, letting the scent fill your lungs. Hold gently for four. Release slowly through the mouth for eight counts, feeling gravity pull you deeper into your seat with each exhale.

layer_13_narrative.text:
16 drops of black pepper carry the dominant β-Caryophyllene through the room slowly — the way Higher Heights intended when they cultivated Amaretto Mintz in Comptche, Mendocino County. The lineage reads Cherry Punch X Animal Mintz, but what arrives in the glass is something that transcends parentage: mint, almond paste, and hint of cherry.

The second oil — Lemon — adds 12 drops of brightness that matches the almond paste note in the flower itself. Physically Relaxed is not a command here but an invitation, the way the best strains suggest rather than insist. The body settles into calm with the kind of molecular precision that only terpenes at 1.48% total can deliver.

In the dimly lit room, the essential oil blend has been working for thirty minutes before the flower arrives. The pre-load creates a terpene environment that the cannabis completes rather than initiates — your olfactory system already tuned to the frequency of β-Caryophyllene, already primed for marzipan calm with a joyful mint finish. The third oil adds depth. The fourth oil rounds everything into a finish that feels inevitable rather than constructed.

This is what Higher Heights grows in Comptche, Mendocino County: not just cannabis, but context. The indica genetics express through terpenes that speak the same chemical language as rosemary in a Mendocino garden. The session settles into its final form — joyful, unhurried, complete — and the 4 oils that started it are now indistinguishable from the flower that finished it.

layer_14_location.terpene_walk: Begin at SF Botanical Garden — Fragrance Garden, diffuse essential oil blend 30 minutes before arrival. Walk slowly, breathing deeply through the nose to layer forest phytoncides over the pre-loaded terpene profile.

wine.terpene_bridge: Rotundone in Syrah shares the spicy, peppery character of β-caryophyllene — same aromatic family, different kingdoms.
wine.tasting_note: Dark fruit, cracked pepper, coastal minerality

wine.terpene_bridge: Zinfandel's brambly spice and warm pepper notes echo caryophyllene's warming, anti-inflammatory character.

beer.terpene_bridge: Belgian yeast esters produce spicy, peppery phenolics that echo caryophyllene's warming quality.
Centennial hops carry significant caryophyllene — cannabis and hops are Cannabaceae cousins.

layer_15_wine_beer.cannabaceae_note: Cannabis and hops (Humulus lupulus) are botanical cousins in the family Cannabaceae, sharing terpene synthesis pathways that produce identical aromatic molecules.
